summary: DeVault uses satirical provocation—offering to pay developers to delete their trivial npm modules without warning—to highlight the software security crisis created by npm's dependency culture. He proposes a formula rewarding deletions of high-download, low-line-count packages to maximize disruption and illustrate the fragility of the ecosystem. After revealing the satire, he argues that most Node developers have never audited their thousands of dependencies, making the entire ecosystem vulnerable to supply chain attacks. He contrasts this with his own practice of keeping dependency counts minimal through deliberate evaluation.
key_points: npm's culture of pulling in thousands of unaudited micro-dependencies for trivial tasks is a serious software security liability
The left-pad incident demonstrated real fragility, and a motivated attacker could exploit the same dynamics through bribery or social engineering
Most developers have no idea what's in their dependency tree and have never audited it
Adding a dependency should require team consensus, auditing, health assessment, and ongoing commitment to re-evaluate
DeVault's own projects typically have fewer than 100 dependencies, many fewer than 10, as a deliberate design choice
money_quotes: It's grossly irresponsible to let dependency trees grow to thousands of dependencies, from vendors you may have never heard of and likely have not critically evaluated, to solve trivial tasks which could have been done from scratch in mere seconds.
I'll pay you cold hard cash to delete your npm module.
A condition of this is that you must delete it without notice, so that everyone who depends on it wakes up to a broken build.
What are you going to do if one of your dependencies vanishes?
What if someone studies the minified code on your website, picks out an obscure dependency they find there, then bribes the maintainers?
Most Node developers have no idea what's in their dependency tree. Most of them are thousands of entries long, and have never been audited.

You can't have a free lunch, I'm afraid. Adding a dependency is a serious decision which requires consensus within the team, an audit of the new dependency, an understanding of its health and long-term prospects, and an ongoing commitment to re-audit them and be prepared to change course as necessary.
key_insight: The npm ecosystem's culture of unaudited micro-dependencies has created a massive, largely invisible supply chain security crisis that developers ignore at their peril.
